caption_text: Every year, MIT releases undergraduate admissions decisions on Pi Day, March 14, in the spirit of the mathematical constant. Decisions for the Class of 2030 will be released on 3/14 at 1:59 p.m. EDT in the year '26, which follows the sequence of digits in pi. Located at the Charles River Esplanade in Boston across from the Institute, this plaque next to a park bench features the first 100-plus digits of pi. Can you spot tomorrow’s date and decision release time? Happy pre Pi Day, everyone!
accessibility_caption: A plaque showing around 100 digits of pi and park bench.

caption_text:
Through a collaboration between MIT and the Museum of Fine Arts, Boston, researchers are creating playable replicas of historical musical instruments. “We want to understand the people who made these instruments through both the materials that they’re made of, but also the sound that they have,” senior postdoc Benjamin Sabatini says.

🔗 in bio for MIT news article
accessibility_caption: Vibration measurement showing an impact hammer, microphone, and green point from a laser Doppler vibrometer.

caption_text:
The official design of the Class of 2028 Brass Rat (@brassrat28) has been revealed! This past Sunday at Ring Premiere, the Class of 2028 Ring Committee shared the traditions, symbols, and easter eggs hidden in the ring with the sophomore class.

As a long-standing tradition since 1929, the Brass Rat serves an emblem celebrating the student body’s time and commitment to MIT.

For more information about the Class of 2028 Brass Rat, see the link in our bio.

Committee Photo: @itqwav

As the 2026 Winter Olympic Games come to a close, we look back at MIT Professor of the practice Carlo Ratti carrying the torch he designed through the streets of Turin, Italy, earlier this January. 

Photo: Milano Cortina 2026
accessibility_caption: Carlo Ratti in a white outfit carrying an Olympic torch, leading a group of individuals in matching jackets down a city street. Emergency vehicle lights flash in the background as they proceed in a night setting.

user.full_name: MIT School of Architecture and Planning

coauthor_producers.full_name: MIT School of Architecture and Planning

caption_text:
Ever wondered what a quantum engineer actually does in a day? ⚛️

Dr. Holly Stemp, a postdoctoral researcher at MIT’s RLE, shares a glimpse — moving between team meetings and lab work, preparing and testing quantum devices at extremely low temperatures.

It’s a behind-the-scenes look at the precision, collaboration, and problem-solving that go into building quantum computers.

#QuantumEngineering #ResearchLife #GraduateStudies #STEMCareers #WomenInSTEM #AcademicLife #ScienceAndEngineering #RLE #MIT

caption_text:
This year’s torches for the Winter Olympic and Paralympic Games were designed by MIT Professor of the Practice Carlo Ratti.

A native of Turin, Italy, and a globally respected designer and architect, Ratti approached the design much as he does when guiding students in his lab. “It is about what the object or the design is to convey,” Ratti says. “How it can touch people, how it can relate to people, how it can transmit emotions. That’s the most important thing.

See 🔗 in bio to read the MIT News article.

#winterolympics #winterolympics2026
accessibility_caption: The torches for the 2026 winter Olympic Games and Paralympic Games.

caption_text:
A quiet return to classes. Welcome back, MIT students!

📷 : Richard Oti/MIT student
accessibility_caption: A person with a backpack walks up the concrete steps toward the entrance Building 7.

caption_text:
Spring, we’re ready when you are!

Image: Jenny Baek '25/ MIT
accessibility_caption: One cartoon groundhog wearing a top hat and one beaver poking out of snow, surrounded by ice and flowers. "Groundhog Day!" is written around them.

caption_text:
Fun with snow on campus! More snow available this weekend. 🌨️☃️❄️

Photos: Jenny Li Fowler/MIT
accessibility_caption: Frog snow sculpture on Hockfield Court.

caption_text:
The Dome, as seen from @mit.nano’s rooftop.

📷: Anton Grassl
accessibility_caption: View of the Dome from MIT.nano rooftop with silmechanical system with large silver HVAC ducts and exhaust stacks in the foreground under a bright blue sky.

caption_text:
During MIT’s Independent Activities Period, MIT community members can step inside MIT.nano’s cleanroom and fabricate their own chip!

Pictured here, participants learn how to operate the PicoTrack, a tool that paints the wafer with a one-micron thin layer of photosensitive material. Next, the wafer goes inside the MLA-150 maskless aligner, which patterns the wafer so that certain parts are exposed to ultraviolet light in the shape of the images. Using a developer, the group dissolves the exposed parts until only the oxide film and shiny aluminum remain. After a trip into a plasma etcher where reactive oxygen removes the photoresist, the wafer is ready to be cleaved into chips.

#nanoscience #nanotechnology #chips #engineering #ThisIsMIT
